title: Single-photon scattering in a dissipative superconducting-qubit--SSH lattice hybrid
authors: Xiao-Xue Zhang, Jie Zhou, Xi-Zheng Zhang
abstract: We study single-photon scattering in a Su--Schrieffer--Heeger (SSH) photonic lattice locally coupled to a superconducting qubit with tunable loss or gain. Working in the single-excitation sector, we derive an explicit real-space scattering formulation for the full energy-dependent scattering matrix $S(E)$ and identify how its eigenvalues encode coherent perfect absorption, amplification, and spectral singular behavior. The analytical results are benchmarked against time-domain wave-packet simulations, which reproduce the stationary scattering probabilities with high accuracy. We show that the SSH dimerization, the qubit-induced non-Hermitian self-energy, and the synthetic gauge phase cooperate to reshape the reflection and transmission spectra in a highly selective way. In particular, changing the dimerization can switch the system between transmission-dominated and reflection-dominated regimes, while the flux provides a direct handle on interference and symmetry-controlled response. We also find a robust loss--gain correspondence in the reflection landscape and show that the linewidth broadening is governed predominantly by the magnitude $|γ|$ of the non-Hermitian coupling. These results establish a compact and experimentally relevant framework for topological scattering in superconducting quantum networks.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical framework to analyze single-photon scattering in a hybrid system combining an SSH photonic lattice and a dissipative/amplifying superconducting qubit. By deriving the energy-dependent scattering matrix $S(E)$, the authors show that topological features (dimerization, flux) combined with non-Hermitian coupling allow for precise control over light transmission and reflection. This establishes a compact model for realizing tunable topological absorbers and amplifiers in quantum networks.
main_problem: The study investigates single-photon scattering dynamics when a topological photonic lattice (SSH) is locally coupled to a superconducting qubit exhibiting tunable loss or gain.
main_result: The system's reflection and transmission spectra are highly controllable by tuning the SSH dimerization, synthetic magnetic flux, and the non-Hermitian coupling strength ($\gamma$).
method: The authors derived an explicit real-space scattering matrix $S(E)$ formulation and benchmarked its analytical results against time-domain wave-packet simulations.
model_or_system: The model combines a Su–Schrieffer–Heeger (SSH) photonic lattice Hamiltonian with a superconducting qubit Hamiltonian, leading to a total system governed by non-Hermitian dynamics in the single-excitation sector.
key_observables: Reflection probability ($R$), Transmission probability ($T$), and the eigenvalues of the scattering matrix $S(E)$.
important_parameters: SSH dimerization ($\delta$), synthetic gauge phase ($\Phi$), and the qubit non-Hermitian coupling magnitude $|\gamma|$ (controlling loss/gain).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to the single-excitation sector, and the scattering problem is solved using asymptotic matching techniques.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate reflection landscapes ($R^{(s)}_L$) vs. momentum $k$ for varying gain/loss ($\gamma$), wave-packet evolution in transmission/reflection regimes, and the eigenvalue spectrum of $S(E)$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from deriving the general scattering formalism to specializing it for the SSH lattice structure, finally incorporating the qubit scatterer and analyzing the resulting spectral features.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete framework for engineering topological quantum devices by controlling light-matter interaction using superconducting circuits, directly relevant to photonic quantum information processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of a dissipative (or amplifying) superconducting qubit embedded in the center of the SSH lat- tice. Superconducting qubit simultaneously coupled to both sublattices A and B of the unit cell at position n = 0.
FIG. 2. Reflection landscape for the AB geometry in the Hermitian limit, t = 1 and γ = 0.
FIG. 3. Reflection landscape for weak gain, t = 1 and γ = 0.1.
FIG. 4. Reflection landscape for stronger gain, t = 1 and γ = 0.3.
FIG. 5. Reflection landscape for weak loss, t = 1 and γ = −0.1.
